name: Caesar Salad
description: Crisp romaine lettuce tossed with creamy Caesar dressing, shaved Parmesan, and crunchy garlic croutons.
pairingReason: crisp greens to cut through rich, heavy mains

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Preheat your oven to 375°F. While it heats, tear or cut day-old bread into rough 1-inch cubes — irregular shapes get crispier edges. Toss with 2 tablespoons olive oil and a pinch of salt. This should take about 3 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't use fresh, soft bread — it won't crisp properly. Day-old or even stale bread makes the best croutons.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Tearing the bread by hand instead of cutting creates more nooks and crannies that catch the dressing.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Spread the bread cubes in a single layer on a baking sheet. Bake at 375°F for 8-10 minutes, tossing halfway through, until golden brown and crunchy throughout.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Croutons go from golden to burnt fast. Check at the 8-minute mark and pull them if they're golden — they'll crisp more as they cool.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While croutons bake, make the dressing. In a medium bowl, whisk together 2 minced garlic cloves, 2 teaspoons Dijon mustard, 2 tablespoons fresh lemon juice, 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, and a pinch of salt.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For an anchovy-free version, the Worcestershire sauce plus a bit of soy sauce gives you that same savory depth.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Caesar salad was invented in Tijuana, Mexico by Italian-American restaurateur Caesar Cardini in 1924 — not in Italy.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While whisking constantly, slowly drizzle in 1/3 cup extra virgin olive oil in a thin stream until the dressing is thick and emulsified. Stir in 1/2 cup finely grated Parmesan. Taste and adjust salt, lemon, or pepper.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Adding the oil too fast will break the emulsion, giving you a thin, separated dressing instead of a creamy one.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Wash and thoroughly dry 2 heads of romaine. Tear into large, bite-sized pieces — damp leaves will dilute the dressing and make everything soggy.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Wet lettuce is the enemy of good Caesar salad. Use a salad spinner or pat leaves dry with clean towels.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Place the romaine in a large bowl, add about 2/3 of the dressing, and toss with your hands until every leaf is lightly coated. Add croutons and toss again. Finish with shaved Parmesan, a crack of black pepper, and extra dressing on the side.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use a vegetable peeler on a block of Parmesan for beautiful, thin shavings rather than grated cheese.

name: Garlic Bread
description: Golden-toasted baguette slices brushed with garlic butter and fresh herbs, crisp on the outside and soft within.
pairingReason: warm bread to soak up sauces and juices

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Preheat your oven to 425°F. Take 1/2 cup (1 stick) unsalted butter out of the fridge and let it soften on the counter for about 10 minutes, or microwave in 10-second bursts until just soft enough to stir — not melted.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Melted butter will soak into the bread and make it greasy instead of forming that crispy, buttery crust. It should be soft but still spreadable.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Mince 4 cloves of fresh garlic finely. In a bowl, mix the softened butter with the garlic, 2 tablespoons chopped fresh parsley, and 1/4 teaspoon salt until well combined.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For mellower garlic flavor with no sharp bite, gently cook the minced garlic in the butter on the stovetop over low heat for 2 minutes before mixing in the herbs.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Italian-American garlic bread as we know it didn't exist in Italy — it evolved from fettunta, Tuscan bread rubbed with raw garlic and drizzled with new olive oil.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Slice a large baguette or Italian loaf in half lengthwise. Place both halves cut-side up on a foil-lined baking sheet.

Spread the garlic butter generously and evenly over the cut sides of the bread, making sure to reach all the way to the edges. Bake at 425°F for 10-12 minutes until the edges are golden and the butter is sizzling.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't skimp on butter at the edges — those spots will dry out and get tough instead of crispy.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Optional: for cheesy garlic bread, sprinkle shredded mozzarella over the top and return to the oven for 2-3 minutes until melted and bubbly. Slice into pieces and serve immediately while hot.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For extra crunch, switch to the broiler for the last 1-2 minutes. Watch it closely — broilers work fast.

name: Tomato Soup
description: A velvety smooth soup made from ripe tomatoes, onion, and a swirl of cream, perfect for dipping bread into.
pairingReason: comforting warmth alongside cheesy or baked dishes

name: Mixed Green Salad
description: A light, refreshing medley of baby greens, cherry tomatoes, cucumber, and a zesty vinaigrette.
pairingReason: light freshness to balance richer proteins

name: Bruschetta
description: Toasted ciabatta topped with a vibrant mix of diced tomatoes, fresh basil, garlic, and a drizzle of balsamic glaze.
pairingReason: bright tomato acidity to complement Italian mains

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Cut 4 medium ripe tomatoes in half and gently scoop out the watery seeds with a spoon. Dice into roughly 1cm (1/2 inch) pieces. Place in a bowl with 1/4 cup roughly chopped fresh basil, 1.5 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil, 1/4 teaspoon salt, and a few grinds of black pepper.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the seeding step results in watery bruschetta that makes the bread soggy. Take the extra minute to remove the seeds.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Bruschetta comes from the Italian "bruscare" meaning "to toast over coals." The topping was originally just olive oil and garlic on grilled bread — tomatoes came later.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Gently toss the tomato mixture and let it sit at room temperature for 10 minutes so the salt draws out flavor and the ingredients meld together.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Salting the tomatoes ahead of time is the secret — it intensifies their sweetness and creates a flavorful juice that soaks into the bread.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Preheat your broiler to high. Slice a baguette into 1/2-inch thick rounds. Brush one side of each slice with olive oil and arrange oil-side up on a baking sheet.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Cut the bread on a slight diagonal for longer, more elegant slices that hold more topping.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Broil the bread slices about 6 inches from the heat for 2-3 minutes until the tops are golden and crisp. Watch them closely — broilers can burn bread in seconds.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Never walk away from the broiler. Bread goes from golden to charred in under 30 seconds at broiler temperatures.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While the toasts are still warm, rub the top of each one lightly with the cut side of a halved garlic clove. The rough, toasted surface acts like a grater, releasing garlic flavor into the bread.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: One garlic clove is enough for about 8 slices. You want a whisper of garlic, not an overpowering hit.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Spoon the marinated tomato mixture generously onto each toast, including some of the juices. Drizzle with extra virgin olive oil and serve immediately — bruschetta gets soggy fast.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Assemble right before serving. If the topping sits on the bread for more than 5 minutes, it becomes a soggy mess.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Finish with a few flakes of Maldon sea salt and a drizzle of aged balsamic for restaurant-quality bruschetta.

name: Minestrone
description: A hearty Italian vegetable soup brimming with beans, pasta, zucchini, and tomatoes in a savoury broth.
pairingReason: hearty vegetable soup for a complete Italian meal

name: Caprese Salad
description: Thick slices of ripe tomato and creamy mozzarella layered with fresh basil, olive oil, and a pinch of sea salt.
pairingReason: fresh mozzarella and tomato to echo Italian flavours

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Remove your fresh mozzarella from the refrigerator 20-30 minutes before assembling. Room temperature mozzarella is more flavorful and has a better, creamier texture than cold.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using cold mozzarella straight from the fridge mutes its delicate milky flavor. Give it time to warm up.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: True caprese salad comes from the island of Capri off the coast of Naples, where it was created as a tribute to the Italian flag — red tomatoes, white mozzarella, green basil.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Select the ripest, most fragrant tomatoes you can find — smell the stem end, which should be earthy and sweet. Using a sharp knife, slice tomatoes into 1/4-inch thick rounds. Lightly sprinkle each slice with fine sea salt and let rest for 10 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't use iodized table salt — it has a slightly bitter taste that sticks out in such a simple dish. Use fine sea salt or kosher salt.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Salting tomatoes draws out moisture and concentrates their natural sweetness. This is the single biggest trick for great caprese.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Slice the mozzarella into rounds of similar thickness to the tomatoes. If using bocconcini (small mozzarella balls), simply halve them.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Dip your knife in warm water between slices to prevent the cheese from sticking and tearing.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Arrange alternating slices of tomato and mozzarella on a serving plate, overlapping them slightly. Tuck fresh basil leaves between the slices.

Drizzle generously with your best extra virgin olive oil. Finish with flaky sea salt (like Maldon), freshly cracked black pepper, and a drizzle of balsamic glaze if desired. Serve immediately at room temperature.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't dress this ahead of time — the salt and oil will break down the tomatoes and make everything watery if it sits too long.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use the best olive oil you own here. In a dish this simple, every ingredient is on full display.

name: Arancini
description: Crispy deep-fried risotto balls filled with gooey mozzarella, coated in golden breadcrumbs and served piping hot.
pairingReason: crispy fried bite for textural contrast

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 tablespoons olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add 1 finely diced onion and 2 minced garlic cloves. Cook for about 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until softened and fragrant but not browned.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Arancini ("little oranges" in Sicilian) date back to 10th-century Sicily. Their name comes from their golden, orange-like appearance after frying.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Add 1.5 cups Arborio rice and stir for 1 minute until the grains are coated and slightly translucent at the edges. Add 3.5 cups chicken stock, 1 tablespoon tomato paste, 1 tablespoon butter, salt, pepper, and a handful of chopped parsley. Bring to a gentle boil.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Arborio rice is essential here — its high starch content makes the balls hold together. Regular long-grain rice will fall apart.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 20-25 minutes until the rice is tender and all the liquid is absorbed. Stir in 1/2 cup finely grated Parmesan. Spread the rice onto a baking sheet and let it cool for at least 15 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: The rice MUST be completely cool before forming balls. Warm rice is too sticky to shape and the cheese filling will melt before you can bread them.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Speed up cooling by spreading the rice as thin as possible on the sheet and placing it in the fridge for 10 minutes.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Set up your breading station: one bowl with flour seasoned with salt and pepper, one bowl with 2 beaten eggs, and one bowl with panko breadcrumbs. Cut mozzarella into 1/2-inch cubes and keep them cold.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Panko breadcrumbs give a much crispier coating than regular breadcrumbs. They're worth seeking out.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: With dampened hands, scoop about 2 tablespoons of rice and flatten it in your palm. Press a mozzarella cube into the center and close the rice around it, forming a tight ball about the size of a golf ball. Repeat to make about 18 balls.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: If the rice sticks to your hands, re-wet them. Dry hands are the #1 reason rice balls fall apart.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use a small cookie scoop to portion the rice evenly — each ball will be the same size and cook at the same rate.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Roll each ball in flour (shake off excess), dip in beaten egg, then coat thoroughly in panko breadcrumbs. Press the breadcrumbs on gently to adhere. Place finished balls on a parchment-lined baking sheet.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the flour step means the egg won't stick, which means the breadcrumbs won't stick, and you'll get bald spots after frying.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 inches of vegetable or canola oil in a heavy-bottomed pot to 350°F. Use a thermometer to verify — temperature is everything here. Fry 4-6 arancini at a time for 3-4 minutes, turning gently, until deep golden brown all over.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: If the oil is too hot (above 375°F), the outside burns before the cheese melts inside. If too cool (below 325°F), the coating absorbs oil and gets greasy.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: No thermometer? Drop a small breadcrumb into the oil — it should sizzle steadily and turn golden in about 30 seconds at 350°F.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Transfer the fried arancini to a wire rack or paper towel-lined plate. Let them rest for 5 minutes — the cheese inside is molten hot. Serve with warm marinara sauce for dipping.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: The filling is dangerously hot right out of the fryer. Give them the full 5 minutes to cool slightly or you'll burn your mouth.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A wire rack keeps the bottoms crispy. Paper towels work but can make the underside a bit soggy.

name: Naan Bread
description: Soft, pillowy Indian flatbread baked in a tandoor oven, brushed with butter and perfect for scooping up curries.
pairingReason: warm bread to scoop up rich curry sauces

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a small bowl, combine 1 teaspoon instant yeast, 1/2 cup warm water (about 105°F), and 1 tablespoon sugar. Stir gently, cover, and let sit for 10 minutes until foamy and bubbly on top.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: If the water is too hot (above 120°F), it will kill the yeast. Use a thermometer or test on your wrist — it should feel warm, not hot.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Instant yeast produces slightly fluffier naan than active dry. If using active dry, let it proof for 15 minutes instead of 10.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a large bowl, sift together 1 3/4 cups bread flour and 1/2 teaspoon salt. Make a well in the center. Add the yeast mixture, 2 tablespoons milk, and 1 1/2 tablespoons beaten egg. Mix until a shaggy dough forms, then knead on a floured surface for 5-6 minutes until smooth and slightly tacky.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't add too much extra flour while kneading — a slightly sticky dough makes softer naan. The dough should spring back when poked.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Bread flour gives a chewier, more bubbly naan than all-purpose. If using all-purpose, the naan will still be good but slightly less pillowy.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Lightly oil the dough ball and place it back in the bowl. Cover with a damp towel or plastic wrap and let rise in a warm spot for 60-90 minutes until doubled in size.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: If your kitchen is cold, preheat the oven to 200°F, turn it off, then place the covered bowl inside. The residual warmth speeds up the rise.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Traditional naan is cooked in a tandoor oven at 900°F, where it sticks to the clay walls and bakes in under 2 minutes. The stovetop method mimics that intense direct heat.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Punch down the dough and divide into 6 equal pieces. Roll each into a smooth ball, place on a lightly floured tray, cover loosely, and let rest for 15 minutes.
On a lightly floured surface, roll each ball into an oval or teardrop shape about 6 inches long and 3-4mm thick. Don't worry about perfection — irregular shapes are traditional.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Rolling too thin makes cracker-like naan. Too thick and the inside stays doughy. Aim for about 3-4mm — roughly the thickness of two stacked coins.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat a cast-iron skillet over high heat until it's smoking hot — about 2 minutes. Place one naan on the dry skillet. Cook for 1-1.5 minutes until the bottom is deep golden with charred spots and large bubbles form on top.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: If the pan isn't hot enough, the naan won't bubble and will turn out flat and tough. Wait until you see wisps of smoke before adding the dough.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Flip the naan and cook the second side for about 1 minute until golden brown with charred spots. Repeat with remaining dough, adjusting heat between medium-high and high to prevent burning.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Keep finished naan warm by wrapping in a clean kitchen towel while you cook the rest. They steam each other and stay soft.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Immediately brush each hot naan generously with melted ghee or butter. Optionally sprinkle with nigella seeds, minced garlic, or chopped cilantro. Serve warm.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For garlic naan, mix 2 minced garlic cloves into the melted ghee before brushing. The residual heat cooks the garlic just enough.

name: Basmati Rice
description: Long-grain aromatic rice steamed to fluffy perfection, with each grain separate and delicately fragrant.
pairingReason: fluffy base to absorb and carry bold spices

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Place 1 cup basmati rice in a fine-mesh strainer. Rinse under cold running water, swirling gently with your hand, until the water runs clear — usually 4-5 rinses. This removes excess surface starch.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the rinse is the most common cause of gummy, sticky basmati. The starch on the surface glues grains together. Always rinse until clear.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Basmati means 'queen of fragrance' in Hindi. The grains elongate up to twice their length when cooked — more than any other rice variety.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Optional but recommended: soak the rinsed rice in cold water for 20 minutes, then drain completely. Soaking allows the grains to absorb water gradually, so they cook more evenly and stay long and fluffy.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: If you're short on time, you can skip soaking — the rice will still be good, just slightly less elongated. Soaking is the difference between good and great.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Place the drained rice and 1 1/2 cups cold water in a medium saucepan. Set over medium-high heat and bring to a simmer without a lid — you'll see the edges start to bubble and the surface foam slightly.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: The 1:1.5 ratio (1 cup rice to 1.5 cups water) is the key to fluffy, separate grains. More water makes mushy rice.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: As soon as it reaches a simmer, place a tight-fitting lid on the pan and reduce heat to low. Cook for exactly 12 minutes without lifting the lid — the steam trapped inside does the real work.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Do not lift the lid during cooking. Every time you peek, steam escapes and the cooking time extends. Trust the timer.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Remove from heat and let the rice sit covered for 5-10 minutes — this resting period redistributes moisture so every grain is evenly fluffy. Then remove the lid and gently fluff with a fork, never a spoon.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using a spoon to fluff will smash the delicate grains. A fork separates them gently without breaking them.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For extra aroma, add a cinnamon stick, 2 cardamom pods, and a bay leaf to the water before cooking. Remove before serving.

name: Raita
description: A cool, creamy yogurt condiment with grated cucumber, cumin, and fresh mint that tempers the heat of spicy dishes.
pairingReason: cool yoghurt to calm the heat of spice

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Peel and grate 1 medium cucumber using the large holes of a box grater. Gather the grated cucumber in a clean kitchen towel, twist tightly, and squeeze out as much liquid as possible over the sink.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: If you skip squeezing the cucumber, the raita will turn watery within minutes. This is the single most important step.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: English (seedless) cucumbers have less water than regular ones, so they need less squeezing. They're ideal for raita.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Raita has been part of Indian cuisine for centuries — the cooling yogurt was traditionally used to balance the heat of spicy dishes, a principle rooted in Ayurvedic food philosophy.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a medium bowl, whisk 1 cup full-fat plain yogurt until smooth. Add the squeezed cucumber, 1/2 teaspoon roasted cumin powder, 1/4 teaspoon chaat masala (if available), a pinch of salt, and a pinch of sugar.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: To roast cumin powder at home: toast 1 tablespoon whole cumin seeds in a dry pan over medium heat for 2 minutes until fragrant and slightly darkened, then grind. The flavor difference is huge.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Add 2 tablespoons finely chopped fresh cilantro and 1 tablespoon finely chopped fresh mint (if available). For heat, mince 1 small green chili (Thai or serrano) and fold it in.

Gently fold everything together until just combined — don't over-mix or the yogurt will thin out. Taste and adjust salt. Cover and refrigerate for at least 15 minutes to let the flavors meld. Serve chilled.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Vigorous stirring breaks down the yogurt's structure and makes raita thin and runny. Use gentle folding motions.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A pinch of kala namak (Himalayan black salt) adds an authentic tangy, sulfurous depth that regular salt can't replicate. Find it at Indian grocery stores.

name: Mango Chutney
description: A sweet and tangy preserve made from ripe mangoes, vinegar, sugar, and warming spices like ginger and mustard seed.
pairingReason: sweet-tangy contrast to savoury curries

name: Samosa
description: A crispy, golden-fried pastry triangle stuffed with spiced potatoes, peas, and cumin, served with tamarind chutney.
pairingReason: crispy spiced pastry to start the meal

name: Aloo Gobi
description: Tender potatoes and cauliflower florets gently spiced with turmeric, cumin, and coriander in a dry-style curry.
pairingReason: dry vegetable dish to complement saucy mains

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Cut 1 small head of cauliflower into small, bite-sized florets (about 1/2 inch). Peel 2 medium potatoes and dice into 1/2-inch cubes. Keep the potatoes submerged in a bowl of water until ready to use to prevent browning.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Cut the potatoes and cauliflower to similar sizes so they cook at the same rate. Large, uneven pieces mean some will be mushy while others are still raw.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Aloo Gobi is one of the most popular vegetarian dishes in North India and Pakistan. 'Aloo' means potato and 'gobi' means cauliflower in Hindi.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 tablespoons oil (mustard oil is traditional) in a large heavy-bottomed pan or wok over medium-high heat. Drain the potatoes, pat dry, and add to the hot oil. Pan-fry for 3-4 minutes, stirring occasionally, until light golden spots appear. Remove and set aside.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Dry the potatoes thoroughly before adding to hot oil — wet potatoes will splatter dangerously and won't brown properly.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In the same pan, add the cauliflower florets and pan-fry over medium-high heat for 2-3 minutes until they develop a few golden-brown spots. Remove and set aside with the potatoes.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Pre-frying the vegetables separately is the secret to great aloo gobi. It seals the outside so they hold their shape instead of turning to mush in the spice mixture.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Add 2 teaspoons oil to the same pan. Once hot, add 1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds and let them sizzle for 10 seconds. Add 1 finely chopped onion and cook for 2-3 minutes until translucent. Stir in 1 1/2 teaspoons ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute until the raw smell disappears.

Add 2 finely chopped tomatoes and cook for 2-3 minutes until soft and pulpy. Then add the spices: 1/2 teaspoon turmeric, 1/4 teaspoon red chili powder, 1 teaspoon coriander powder, and 1/2 teaspoon amchur (dry mango powder). Stir well for 30 seconds.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Add the ground spices after the tomatoes have softened — raw spices in dry heat burn instantly and taste bitter.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Return the pan-fried potatoes and cauliflower to the pan. Add 3/4 teaspoon salt, mix gently to coat everything in the spice mixture, and sprinkle 2-3 tablespoons of water around the edges of the pan. Cover and cook on low heat for 8-10 minutes, stirring gently every 2 minutes, until the vegetables are fork-tender but not mushy.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Stir gently — aggressive stirring will break the cauliflower into crumbs. And don't overcook — the vegetables should have a slight bite, not fall apart.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: If the mixture sticks to the pan, add water 1 tablespoon at a time. You want a dry dish, not a curry, so use water sparingly.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Remove from heat and sprinkle with 1/4 teaspoon garam masala and a generous pinch of crushed kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). Cover and let sit for 5 minutes — the residual heat blooms the final spices. Garnish with chopped cilantro and a squeeze of lemon juice before serving.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Crushing kasuri methi between your palms before adding it releases far more aroma than sprinkling it whole. This one ingredient transforms the dish.

name: Dal Tadka
description: Creamy yellow lentils simmered until soft and finished with a sizzling temper of cumin, garlic, and dried chillies.
pairingReason: protein-rich lentils to round out the plate

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Rinse 1 cup chana dal (yellow split peas) in cold water until it runs clear. Soak in fresh water for 1 hour, then drain. This softens the lentils and cuts the cooking time nearly in half.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the soak means the dal will take much longer to cook and may not break down to the right creamy consistency.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: In a hurry? Use a mix of red lentils (masoor dal) and yellow lentils (toor dal) — they cook in 20-25 minutes without soaking.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Dal is the most eaten dish in India — nearly 60 million tons of pulses are consumed annually. Every region has its own signature dal recipe.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 tablespoons ghee in a heavy-bottomed saucepan over high heat. Add 2 deseeded green chilies (cut into chunks) and fry for about 1 minute until they start to blister.

Add 1 finely chopped onion and cook over medium heat for 3-4 minutes until softened and translucent. Then add 6 finely chopped garlic cloves, 1 tablespoon finely chopped ginger, and 8 fresh curry leaves. Stir for about 1 minute until the garlic turns golden and the kitchen smells incredible.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Burnt garlic tastes bitter and will ruin the dal. Keep the heat at medium and stir constantly once you add it.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Add 1 chopped tomato and 1/2 teaspoon ground cumin. Cook for 2 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomato breaks down into a paste-like consistency.

Add the drained lentils, 4 cups water, 1/2 teaspoon turmeric, and 3/4 teaspoon salt. Bring to a simmer, then cover and cook gently on low heat for about 1 hour, stirring 2-3 times to prevent sticking.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Keep the heat low — a gentle simmer. Rapid boiling can cause the dal to stick and burn on the bottom.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Remove the lid and continue simmering gently for 20-30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the dal reaches a thick, porridge-like consistency. Some lentils should be broken down while others hold their shape. Stir in 1/8 teaspoon garam masala.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use a whisk or the back of a wooden spoon to mash some of the lentils against the side of the pot — this creates a creamier texture while leaving some whole lentils for texture contrast.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Make the tadka: In a small pan, heat 1 1/2 tablespoons ghee over medium-high heat until shimmering. Add 1 teaspoon cumin seeds and 1/2 teaspoon black mustard seeds — they should crackle and pop within 20 seconds. Add 3 dried red chilies (seeds removed) and stir for 10 seconds, then add a sliced shallot and cook until lightly golden, about 1 minute.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: The tadka happens fast — have all your spices measured and within arm's reach before heating the ghee. Once the mustard seeds start popping, you have seconds before the spices burn.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: 'Tadka' (also called tempering or 'chaunk') is a technique where whole spices are bloomed in hot fat to release their essential oils. It's what gives dal its signature nutty, aromatic finish.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Immediately pour the sizzling tadka over the cooked dal — it should hiss and spit dramatically. Stir through gently. Garnish with fresh cilantro and serve over steamed basmati rice or with warm naan.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Pour the tadka into the dal, not the other way around. The narrow stream of hot fat hitting the broad surface of the dal creates more flavor distribution.

name: Papadum
description: Thin, crispy lentil wafers that shatter satisfyingly with each bite, often served with pickles and chutneys.
pairingReason: shattering crunch to contrast soft curries

name: Guacamole
description: Ripe avocados mashed with lime juice, cilantro, diced onion, and a kick of jalapeño for a creamy, zesty dip.
pairingReason: creamy richness to cool spicy fillings

name: Mexican Rice
description: Fluffy rice toasted until golden, then simmered with tomatoes, garlic, and chicken broth for a savoury side.
pairingReason: savoury tomato rice to soak up bold salsas

name: Elote
description: Chargrilled corn on the cob slathered with creamy mayo, cotija cheese, chilli powder, and a squeeze of lime.
pairingReason: charred sweetness to balance smoky meats

name: Pico de Gallo
description: A fresh, chunky salsa of diced tomatoes, white onion, jalapeño, cilantro, and lime juice — bright and vibrant.
pairingReason: bright, raw freshness to lift heavy dishes

name: Refried Beans
description: Pinto beans cooked until tender, then mashed and fried with onion and lard for a rich, creamy, savoury side.
pairingReason: creamy protein to make the meal substantial

name: Churros
description: Golden, ridged sticks of fried dough rolled in cinnamon sugar, crispy outside and soft inside, served with chocolate sauce.
pairingReason: sweet finish to close a savoury meal

name: Esquites
description: Mexican street corn kernels served in a cup with mayo, cotija cheese, chilli, lime, and a sprinkle of tajín.
pairingReason: tangy street-food corn for textural variety

name: Tortilla Chips
description: Thin wedges of corn tortilla fried or baked until perfectly crunchy, ideal for scooping salsa, guac, or queso.
pairingReason: crunchy scoop for dips and salsas alongside

name: Gyoza
description: Japanese pan-fried dumplings with a crispy golden bottom and juicy pork-and-cabbage filling, served with soy-vinegar dip.
pairingReason: crispy-bottomed dumplings for a savoury starter

name: Coleslaw
description: Crunchy shredded cabbage and carrots tossed in a creamy, tangy dressing with a hint of celery seed.
pairingReason: cool crunch to offset smoky, grilled meats

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Shred 1 medium head (about 2 lbs) of green cabbage as finely as you can with a sharp knife or mandoline. Grate 2 carrots on the large holes of a box grater at a slight angle so the strands are long. Thinly slice ½ a small red onion.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Cutting cabbage too thick gives you a chunky, water-logged slaw. Aim for thin ribbons no wider than a pencil lead.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Grating carrots at a 45° angle along the grater gives you longer, more elegant strands that toss into the cabbage instead of falling to the bottom.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Toss the cabbage, carrots, and onion in a colander set over a bowl with 2 tsp kosher salt and 2 tsp sugar. Let sit exactly 5 minutes — this purges the water that would otherwise make your slaw soupy.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Going past 5 minutes turns the cabbage limp. Set a timer.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Salt and sugar both pull water out via osmosis. The sugar also pre-seasons the cabbage so it tastes balanced.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Rinse the cabbage thoroughly under cold water to wash off the salt, then spread it on clean kitchen towels and pat dry. Do not squeeze — squeezing crushes the texture.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the rinse leaves the slaw too salty. Skipping the dry leaves it watery the next day.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A salad spinner works even better than towels for this step.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a large bowl, whisk ⅓ cup mayonnaise, 2 Tbsp Dijon mustard, 2 Tbsp apple cider vinegar, 1 Tbsp sugar, ½ tsp celery seed, and a few grinds of black pepper until smooth.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Add 1 Tbsp of buttermilk for a tangier, looser dressing — Kenji's preferred move.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Add the dried cabbage mixture to the dressing and toss with tongs until every shred is coated. Taste and adjust with more salt, pepper, or vinegar.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Slaw tastes best after 30 minutes in the fridge — flavors meld and the cabbage softens just enough.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Cover and refrigerate at least 30 minutes before serving. Slaw keeps 3 days covered.

name: Corn on the Cob
description: Sweet summer corn grilled or boiled until tender, slathered in melted butter and sprinkled with salt.
pairingReason: buttery sweetness alongside barbecue and grills

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Shuck 4–6 ears of corn — strip back the husk and pull off the silk threads. Snap or cut off the stem. Inspect for any browned kernels and trim them.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Run a damp paper towel down the cob from top to bottom — it grabs every last silk strand in one pass.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil over high heat. Use enough water to fully submerge the corn — about 4 quarts for 4 ears.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Salting the boiling water does almost nothing for corn — the cooking time is too short to penetrate. Save the salt for after.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Lower the corn into the boiling water, cover the pot, and immediately turn the heat OFF. Let the corn sit, undisturbed, for 10 minutes. The residual heat cooks it perfectly without overdoing it.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Boiling corn at full heat for 10 minutes makes it rubbery and starchy. The off-heat method is the Serious Eats-tested winner.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Frozen corn works too — add 2 extra minutes.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Lift the corn out with tongs onto a plate. Immediately rub each ear with a pat of softened butter and shower with flaky salt while still steaming hot.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Roll the buttered corn in a shallow plate of grated Parmesan and chopped chives for an instant upgrade.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Sweet corn loses about 50% of its sweetness within 24 hours of picking — the fresher the better.

name: French Fries
description: Twice-fried potato sticks with a shattering golden crust and fluffy interior, generously seasoned with salt.
pairingReason: salty, crispy classic alongside burgers

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Peel 2 lbs (about 4) russet potatoes. Cut into ¼-inch by ¼-inch sticks — uniform thickness is crucial for even cooking. Drop the sticks into a bowl of cold water as you go to prevent browning.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using waxy potatoes (red, Yukon) makes soggy fries. Russets have the right starch content.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Square off the potato first by cutting a thin slab off each side — this gives you uniform sticks instead of awkward curved pieces.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the potatoes and place them in a large pot. Cover with cold water by 1 inch, add 2 Tbsp white vinegar and 2 Tbsp kosher salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a bare simmer for 10 minutes until the fries are nearly tender but hold their shape.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the vinegar makes fries that turn to mush in the boil. The acid keeps the pectin firm.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: This boiled-then-fried method is Kenji's breakthrough — it gelatinizes the surface starches so the fries crisp up instantly when fried.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the fries gently and spread them on a paper-towel-lined baking sheet. Pat dry. Let them air-dry for 5 minutes — surface moisture is the enemy of crispness.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Wet fries make the oil splatter dangerously and create a soggy crust.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 quarts of peanut or canola oil in a Dutch oven to 400°F (use a thermometer). Working in 3 batches, fry the potatoes for 50 seconds per batch — they should be pale and limp when removed. Let oil return to 400°F between batches.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Crowding the oil drops the temperature and gives you greasy fries. Three batches, no shortcuts.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: After this first fry, you can refrigerate the fries for up to a day before the second fry — perfect for entertaining.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Increase oil temperature to 400°F. Fry the potatoes a second time, all together this time, until deep golden brown and crispy, 3–4 minutes. Stir gently with a spider to prevent sticking.
Transfer fries to a paper-towel-lined bowl, immediately season with kosher salt while shiny with oil so the salt sticks. Toss to coat. Serve right now — fries lose their crisp within 5 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Salting after they cool means the salt slides off. Salt while hot.

name: Onion Rings
description: Thick-cut onion slices dipped in seasoned batter and fried until the coating turns crispy and deep golden brown.
pairingReason: sweet-savoury crunch for a diner-style pairing

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Slice 2 large yellow onions into ½-inch-thick rounds. Separate into individual rings, discarding the small inner rings (or save them for soup). You should have about 24 large rings.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Slicing thinner than ½ inch gives you flimsy rings that overcook before the crust browns.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use sweet onions like Vidalia for a milder flavor that lets the crust shine.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Soak the onion rings in 2 cups buttermilk with 1 tsp salt for 30 minutes. The buttermilk tenderizes and adds a subtle tang while the salt seasons the onion itself.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: No buttermilk? Stir 2 Tbsp lemon juice into 2 cups whole milk and let it sit 5 minutes.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a large bowl, whisk together 2 cups all-purpose flour, ½ cup cornstarch, 1 Tbsp baking powder, 2 tsp salt, 1 tsp paprika, and ½ tsp cayenne. The cornstarch is the secret to extra crunch.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: ATK's test was clear: cornstarch + flour beats flour alone every time. The cornstarch fries up shatter-crisp.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 quarts of vegetable oil in a Dutch oven to exactly 375°F. A thermometer is non-negotiable — too low and the rings absorb oil; too high and they burn before cooking through.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Eyeballing oil temperature is the #1 onion ring failure. Get a clip-on thermometer.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Working in batches, lift rings from buttermilk, let excess drip off, then dredge in the flour mixture, pressing firmly so the coating sticks. Re-dip in buttermilk and dredge again for a double coat.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the double-dredge gives you a thin crust that falls off in the oil.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: ATK shakes all the rings together with the flour mix in a paper grocery bag — faster and more even than dredging one by one.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Lower 6–8 rings into the oil with tongs. Fry without stirring for 30–60 seconds to set the crust, then stir gently and continue frying until dark golden brown, 3–5 minutes total.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Stirring too early knocks off the wet coating before it can set.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Transfer to a wire rack set over a baking sheet (not paper towels — they trap steam and soften the crust). Sprinkle with flaky salt immediately. Repeat with remaining rings, returning oil to 375°F between batches.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Resting hot fried food on paper towels is how you ruin onion rings. Always use a wire rack.

name: Sweet Potato Fries
description: Crispy-on-the-outside, soft-inside sweet potato sticks with a natural caramel sweetness and a hint of sea salt.
pairingReason: natural sweetness for a lighter fry option

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 425°F and line two large baking sheets with parchment paper. Hot oven + two pans (so fries have room) is the foundation of crispy oven fries.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Crowding fries onto one pan steams them. Two pans is non-negotiable for crispness.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Peel 2 lbs sweet potatoes (about 2 large) and cut into matchsticks ¼-inch thick on each side. Uniform size = uniform crispness.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Cutting fries thicker than ¼ inch leaves them soft inside while the outside burns.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Square off the round sides of the potato first — easier to get straight cuts from a flat surface.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Soak the cut fries in a bowl of cold water for at least 30 minutes (up to 2 hours). This pulls out surface starches that would otherwise turn gummy.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the soak is the #1 reason home-baked sweet potato fries come out limp.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the fries and dry them THOROUGHLY with kitchen towels — pat each one until no water remains. Toss in a dry bowl with 2 Tbsp cornstarch until every fry is lightly coated.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Wet fries + cornstarch = paste. Dry first, cornstarch second.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: The order matters: oil only AFTER the cornstarch coats. Cornstarch on dry fries → then drizzle 2 Tbsp oil → toss to coat.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drizzle 2 Tbsp olive oil over the cornstarched fries and toss to coat evenly. Season with 1 tsp salt, ½ tsp garlic powder, and ½ tsp smoked paprika. Spread in a single layer across both baking sheets — fries should not touch.
Bake on the center and lower racks for 20 minutes. Pull both pans, flip every fry with a thin metal spatula, and rotate the pans (top to bottom, front to back).
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A thin metal spatula slides under without ripping the cornstarch coating.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Return to the oven and bake another 10–15 minutes, until the fries are deep golden brown at the edges and crisp. Watch the last 5 minutes carefully — sweet potatoes go from perfect to burnt fast.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Sweet potatoes have more sugar than russets and burn quickly. Stay close at the end.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Let the fries cool on the pan for 3–5 minutes — they continue to crisp as they cool. Transfer to a serving plate and add a final sprinkle of flaky salt.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Serve with chipotle aioli: ½ cup mayo + 1 minced chipotle in adobo + squeeze of lime.

name: Potato Salad
description: Tender potato chunks folded with mayo, mustard, crunchy celery, hard-boiled eggs, and a scatter of fresh dill.
pairingReason: creamy potato side for outdoor cookouts

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Peel 2 lbs Yukon Gold potatoes and cut into ¾-inch cubes. Place directly into a large pot and cover with cold water by 1 inch. Add 2 Tbsp white vinegar and 2 Tbsp kosher salt.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Starting potatoes in hot water cooks the outsides before the centers — always start in cold water for even cooking.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Vinegar in the boiling water keeps the pectin firm so cubes hold their shape instead of crumbling.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Bring the pot to a boil over high heat, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook until a paring knife slides into a cube with no resistance, 12–15 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Boiling too hard knocks the cubes against each other and shears the edges into a mushy paste. Keep it at a gentle simmer.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the potatoes in a colander. While still hot, toss them gently in a wide bowl with 2 Tbsp white wine vinegar — hot potatoes drink up the seasoning. Let cool 10 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the hot vinegar dousing leaves bland potatoes that no amount of dressing later can fix.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: This step is Kenji's key insight: season at every layer, not just at the end.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While potatoes cool, finely dice 3 ribs of celery, 4 scallions (whites and greens separated), and 4 hard-boiled eggs. Roughly chop ⅓ cup cornichons or dill pickles.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Hard-boil eggs by placing in cold water, bringing to a boil, covering, turning off heat, and waiting 11 minutes. Ice bath, then peel.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a large bowl, whisk ¾ cup mayonnaise, 2 Tbsp Dijon mustard, 2 Tbsp sweet pickle relish, 1 tsp celery seed, ½ tsp salt, and freshly ground black pepper. Fold in the cooled potatoes, celery, scallion whites, eggs, and pickles.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Fold gently with a rubber spatula, lifting from the bottom — stirring smashes the cubes.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Refrigerate for at least 1 hour. Just before serving, taste and adjust salt and pepper, then top with the reserved scallion greens and a sprinkle of paprika.

name: Cornbread
description: Warm, golden cornbread with a slightly sweet crumb and a buttery crust, baked in a cast-iron skillet.
pairingReason: sweet, crumbly bread to mop up barbecue sauce

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Place a 10-inch cast iron skillet in the cold oven and preheat to 425°F. The screaming-hot pan is what gives Southern cornbread its signature crackly crust.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Pouring batter into a cold or barely warm skillet is the #1 reason home cornbread is pale and pasty on the bottom.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: No cast iron? A heavy oven-safe skillet works, but the crust won't be as dramatic.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Cast iron is the traditional Southern vessel for cornbread because the thermal mass mimics a hearth oven.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a large bowl, whisk together 1½ cups yellow cornmeal, ½ cup all-purpose flour, 2 Tbsp sugar (or honey), 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp baking soda, and 1 tsp baking powder.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the baking soda gives you flat, dense cornbread — buttermilk needs both soda (for rise) and powder (for lift).
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use stone-ground cornmeal for a more rustic, full-corn flavor and slightly coarser crumb.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a separate bowl, whisk 2 large eggs with 1¾ cups buttermilk and ¼ cup melted unsalted butter (cooled slightly so it doesn't scramble the eggs).
Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and stir with a rubber spatula just until no streaks of flour remain. Some lumps are fine — overmixing develops gluten and gives you tough cornbread.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Stirring until perfectly smooth = chewy, dense bread. Stop the moment everything is moistened.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pull the hot skillet from the oven (use thick mitts!). Add 2 Tbsp butter — it will sizzle and foam. Swirl to coat the bottom and sides, then immediately pour in the batter. It should sizzle on contact.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: If the butter doesn't sizzle when it hits the pan, the pan isn't hot enough — you'll lose the crust.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Substitute the butter with 2 Tbsp bacon fat for the truly authentic Southern flavor.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Return the skillet to the oven and bake until the top is golden brown and a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean, 20–25 minutes.
Let the cornbread rest in the skillet for 5 minutes before slicing into wedges. Serve warm with butter and honey.

name: Baked Beans
description: Navy beans slow-baked in a smoky, sweet molasses and tomato sauce with bits of bacon and brown sugar.
pairingReason: smoky-sweet beans for a classic barbecue spread

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Soak 1 lb dried navy beans in 8 cups cold water overnight (at least 8 hours). They'll roughly double in size.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the overnight soak adds 1+ hours to the cooking time and gives you unevenly cooked beans.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: No time to soak overnight? Quick-soak: cover beans with water, boil 2 minutes, cover, off heat, sit 1 hour.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Boston Baked Beans built American molasses culture — colonial New England traded heavily in West Indies molasses.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain and rinse the beans. Place in a Dutch oven with 8 cups fresh water. Bring to a boil, reduce to a simmer, and cook uncovered until tender but still holding shape, about 60 minutes. Drain, reserving 2 cups of the cooking liquid.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Adding salt or acid (tomato, vinegar) before the beans are tender keeps them tough forever. Save those for after.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While beans simmer, cut 8 oz thick-cut bacon into ½-inch pieces. In the same Dutch oven (after draining beans), fry the bacon over medium heat until the fat is rendered and bacon is just crisp, 6–8 minutes. Remove bacon to a paper towel; leave fat in the pot.
In the bacon fat, sauté 1 large diced yellow onion until softened and golden at the edges, 6–8 minutes. Add 2 minced garlic cloves and cook 30 seconds more.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Don't rush the onions — the deeper they brown here, the deeper the finished bean flavor.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Stir in ½ cup fancy molasses (NOT blackstrap — too bitter), ⅓ cup brown sugar, ⅓ cup ketchup, 2 Tbsp apple cider vinegar, 2 Tbsp Dijon mustard, 1 tsp salt, and ½ tsp black pepper. Add the cooked beans, bacon, and 1½ cups of the reserved bean liquid. Stir to combine.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Blackstrap molasses turns baked beans bitter and metallic. Use "fancy" or "light" molasses (Grandma's, Crosby's).

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Cover the Dutch oven and transfer to a 350°F oven. Bake 2 hours, stirring every 30 minutes and adding more bean liquid (or water) if it looks dry.
Uncover and bake another 30 minutes, until the sauce is thick and glossy and a few beans on top are slightly caramelized. Let rest 10 minutes before serving — they thicken further off the heat.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Leftovers are even better day two — let them cool completely, then reheat with a splash of water.

name: Mac & Cheese
description: Elbow pasta swimming in a luscious, three-cheese sauce, topped with buttery breadcrumbs and baked until bubbly.
pairingReason: indulgent cheese comfort for hearty meals

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Grate 6 oz sharp cheddar (or a mix of cheddar + Gruyère) on the large holes of a box grater. Pre-grated bagged cheese has anti-caking starch that makes the sauce gritty.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using bagged shredded cheese is the fastest way to ruin this recipe — the cellulose coating refuses to melt smoothly.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A 50/50 mix of sharp cheddar and Gruyère gives you classic flavor + extra meltiness.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Measure 6 oz dried elbow macaroni and 6 oz (¾ cup) evaporated milk into separate small bowls. Have everything within arm's reach — once the cooking starts, it moves fast.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use a kitchen scale — eyeballing 6 oz of dried pasta is hard, and ratios matter for this no-roux method.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Place the macaroni in a medium saucepan and add just enough cold water to cover (about 1½ cups). Add a generous pinch of salt. Bring to a boil over high heat, stirring frequently to prevent sticking.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Adding too much water dilutes the starch you need for a creamy sauce. Just barely cover.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: The starchy pasta water IS the sauce base in this method — that's why traditional "drain the pasta" rules don't apply here.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Continue cooking, stirring constantly, until most of the water has been absorbed and the pasta is just shy of al dente, about 6 minutes total. The pot should look almost dry.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Walking away during this stage will scorch the pasta on the bottom. Constant stirring is non-negotiable.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pour in the evaporated milk and bring back to a simmer. Reduce heat to low and add the grated cheese a handful at a time, stirring continuously until each addition is fully melted before adding the next.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Dumping all the cheese in at once breaks the sauce. Add gradually for a perfectly emulsified, glossy cheese sauce.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: The proteins in evaporated milk are what stabilize this sauce — regular milk will break and turn grainy.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Stir in ½ tsp Dijon mustard, a few grinds of black pepper, and a pinch of cayenne for warmth. Taste and adjust salt. Serve immediately — this dish is best the moment it's done.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Dijon doesn't taste mustardy in the finished dish — it amplifies the cheese flavor.

name: Pickles
description: Crunchy dill pickle spears brined in vinegar, garlic, and dill — a tangy, refreshing palate cleanser.
pairingReason: sharp tang to cut through greasy, rich bites

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Wash 1½ lbs Kirby (pickling) cucumbers and trim about ⅛ inch off each end — the blossom end contains enzymes that soften pickles. Slice into ¼-inch coins or spears.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Regular slicing cucumbers (long, smooth-skinned) give you soft, hollow pickles. Always use Kirby or Persian.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For maximum crunch, soak cut cucumbers in ice water for 30 minutes before brining.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a clean wide-mouth quart jar, layer in 4 smashed garlic cloves, 1 Tbsp whole black peppercorns, 1 tsp yellow mustard seeds, 1 tsp coriander seeds, ½ tsp red pepper flakes, and a generous handful of fresh dill (about ¼ cup of fronds + 2 dill flower heads if you have them).
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Smashing the garlic with the side of a knife (not mincing) releases flavor without making the pickles taste like garlic paste.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pack the cucumbers into the jar, standing them on end if cut as spears, or layering coins. Pack tightly — they should not float freely.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Loosely packed pickles bob to the surface, dry out, and turn slimy.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a small saucepan, combine 1½ cups distilled white vinegar (5% acidity), 1½ cups water, 2 Tbsp kosher salt, and 1 Tbsp sugar. Bring to a boil over high heat, stirring until the salt and sugar fully dissolve, about 2 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using a vinegar with less than 5% acidity won't safely preserve the pickles — check the label.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Remove brine from heat and let cool until just barely warm to the touch (about 15 minutes), or speed it up by setting the pot in an ice bath. Pouring boiling brine over cucumbers makes mushy pickles.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Hot brine = soft pickles, period. Patience here is the difference between snappy and sad.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Refrigerator pickles are NOT shelf-stable — they keep 4–6 weeks in the fridge but should never be left out.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pour the cooled brine over the cucumbers until fully submerged. Tap the jar gently on the counter to release air bubbles. Seal and refrigerate at least 24 hours before tasting — they'll keep getting better for a week.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For peak flavor, wait 3 full days. The garlic and dill need time to infuse.

name: Kimchi
description: Spicy, tangy fermented napa cabbage seasoned with gochugaru, garlic, ginger, and fish sauce — Korea's iconic side.
pairingReason: fermented funk and heat with every Korean meal

name: Korean Pancake
description: A crispy, savoury pancake loaded with scallions, seafood, or kimchi, pan-fried until golden and served with soy dip.
